source.title: Selective suppression of hippocampal ripples impairs spatial memory

confidence_rationale: Girardeau et al. provide direct causal evidence for the necessity of hippocampal SWRs (sharp-wave ripples) during post-training rest for spatial memory consolidation. Using closed-loop detection and stimulation to selectively suppress SWRs during the consolidation period (while leaving overall sleep architecture intact), they showed performance impairment in rats trained on a hippocampus-dependent spatial memory task. This is one of the foundational causal necessity papers: the suppression manipulates the replay vehicle specifically, not sleep as a whole. Directly supports SD-017's claim that the SWS-analog phase (SWR-mediated hippocampus-to-cortex replay) is necessary for hippocampal consolidation. High source quality (Nature Neuroscience). Small n is the main limitation.
failure_signatures: The stimulation disrupts ripples but not perfectly selectively -- partial disruption of co-occurring cortical activity cannot be fully excluded.

The spatial memory task (8-arm radial maze, same 3 baited arms daily) tests reference memory, which is primarily hippocampus-dependent; the result may not generalise to more complex multi-context attribution tasks that SD-017 requires.
The study does not directly test whether SWRs are necessary for causal attribution or slot-filling specifically -- only spatial navigation performance is measured.

mapping.source_claim_statement: Selective closed-loop suppression of hippocampal SPW-Rs during post-training rest periods impairs performance on a hippocampus-dependent spatial reference memory task. Sleep structure is preserved; only SWR events are disrupted.
mapping.ree_translation: SD-017 requires a minimal SWS-analog phase providing bidirectional hippocampus-to-cortex replay for schema/slot consolidation. Girardeau et al. establish the causal necessity claim: removing the SWR vehicle (while leaving sleep intact) prevents consolidation. In REE terms, SWRs carry the hippocampal attribution map updates into the neocortical schema layer (E1 world model). Without SWRs, the hippocampal E3 system accumulates episodic attributions that never transfer to the schematic layer -- the long-term harm/benefit map remains episodic and context-undifferentiated. This is precisely the failure mode SD-017 predicts: without offline SWS-analog phases, hippocampal attribution mapping cannot converge.
mapping.mapping_caveat: The Girardeau study tests spatial memory, not causal attribution or context discrimination specifically. SD-017 requires necessity for the attribution pipeline (hippocampus to schema slot-filling), not just spatial navigation. The gap between spatial memory and attribution remains an inferential step. The V3 analog of this experiment (EXQ-242) tests context discrimination with SWS-phase ablation, which is a closer test of the SD-017 mechanism.
mapping.source_context: Adult rats; 8-arm radial maze with 3 baited arms (same daily); hippocampal tetrode recordings; closed-loop SWR detection and disruption via contralateral fimbria stimulation; comparison of learning rate between control and stimulated sessions.

confidence_components.notes: Nature Neuroscience, Buzsaki lab (definitive authority on SWRs). High source quality. Mapping fidelity moderate: spatial memory consolidation is well supported but the specific attribution/schema consolidation function of SD-017 requires additional inference. Transfer risk moderate: rodent spatial task to REE multi-context attribution.
